original_hypothesis.title: Fee-on-Transfer Token Breaks asdUSDC Solvency Invariant
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: ASDUSDC.deposit records usdcBalances[_usdcVersion] += _amount using the input amount, not the actual received amount after transfer. If a whitelisted USDC version has fee-on-transfer behavior, the contract receives less than _amount but records the full _amount. This inflates usdcBalances above the real balance, causing insolvency — later withdrawers cannot withdraw because the contract holds insufficient tokens.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Project documentation explicitly states the system expects to interact with tokens that may have fee-on-transfer mechanics. The code does not measure actual received amount (balance before vs after pattern). First depositors can extract the fee difference at the expense of later depositors.
deployment_assumptions: A fee-on-transfer USDC version is whitelisted by the owner
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: Project documentation states: 'The system expects to interact with tokens that may have fee-on-transfer mechanics'. No balance-before/after check in deposit().

proof_of_signal.pattern: usdcBalances[_usdcVersion] += _amount after safeTransferFrom; _amount is transfer amount, not received amount

preconditions: Owner whitelists a fee-on-transfer USDC variant
Multiple users deposit the fee-on-transfer token

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: usdcBalances += _amount without measuring actual received amount
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: After safeTransferFrom of fee-on-transfer token: actual balance increase < _amount, but usdcBalances records full _amount
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should use: uint before = ERC20(v).balanceOf(this); transferFrom; uint received = ERC20(v).balanceOf(this) - before;

analysis_questions: Which USDC bridge variants on Canto have fee-on-transfer?
How quickly does the insolvency compound with multiple deposits?
Can an attacker extract value by being an early depositor and withdrawing before insolvency becomes apparent?
validation_requirements: Deploy with fee-on-transfer mock token, deposit twice, verify second withdraw fails
Calculate attacker profit from depositing and immediately withdrawing
Verify recover() cannot fix the discrepancy

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker deposits 1000 fee-on-transfer USDC (1% fee). Contract records 1000 but holds 990. Attacker withdraws 1000 asdUSDC, receives 1000 USDC from pool (using reserves from other depositors). Last depositor cannot withdraw.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Theft of other depositors' USDC proportional to fee rate
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Later depositors of the fee-on-transfer version
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Owner can partially recover via recover() but cannot fix the accounting gap

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Deposit amount proportional to desired theft
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing balanceOf vs usdcBalances

validation_result.impact_summary: If a fee-on-transfer USDC variant is whitelisted, deposit() inflates usdcBalances above actual token holdings, causing insolvency for later withdrawers and breaking recover().
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The ASDUSDC.deposit() function at asdUSDC.sol:36-42 performs safeTransferFrom for _amount and then records usdcBalances[_usdcVersion] += _amount. If the transferred token charges a fee on transfer, the contract receives less than _amount tokens, but the accounting records the full _amount. This creates a permanent discrepancy between the recorded usdcBalances and the actual ERC20 balance held by the contract. Over multiple deposits, this discrepancy compounds. Early withdrawers can withdraw their full recorded amount (draining real tokens), while later withdrawers face a safeTransfer revert when the contract's actual balance is insufficient. Furthermore, the recover() function cannot fix this because it computes amountToRecover = balanceOf(this) - usdcBalances[version], which would underflow (revert in Solidity 0.8) when usdcBalances exceeds the actual balance.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Lines 36-42 of asdUSDC.sol clearly show the vulnerable pattern: safeTransferFrom followed by usdcBalances += _amount without measuring actual received amount. No balance-before/after pattern is used. The withdraw function at lines 53-64 uses safeTransfer which will revert when the contract lacks sufficient tokens. The recover function at lines 71-79 will also revert due to underflow when usdcBalances > actual balance.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: After safeTransferFrom of a fee-on-transfer token, the contract receives less than _amount, but usdcBalances records the full _amount. Should use: uint256 before = ERC20(v).balanceOf(address(this)); safeTransferFrom(...); uint256 received = ERC20(v).balanceOf(address(this)) - before; and use 'received' instead of '_amount' for accounting.
The require check passes (usdcBalances is inflated), but safeTransfer will revert when the actual token balance is insufficient. Last withdrawers are unable to withdraw.

When usdcBalances[_usdcVersion] > ERC20(_usdcVersion).balanceOf(address(this)) due to fee-on-transfer inflation, the subtraction underflows and reverts in Solidity 0.8. The owner cannot use recover() to fix the discrepancy.

calling_sequence: 1. User calls deposit(_usdcVersion, 1000) where _usdcVersion is a fee-on-transfer token (1% fee)
3. safeTransferFrom transfers 1000 from user, contract receives 990 (10 lost to fee) (line 39)
4. usdcBalances[_usdcVersion] += 1000 (records full amount, now inflated by 10) (line 40)
6. Second user deposits 1000, contract receives 990 more (total actual: 1980, recorded: 2000)
7. First user calls withdraw for 1e15 asdUSDC, amountToWithdraw = 1000
9. safeTransfer sends 1000 to first user (actual balance drops to 980, recorded to 1000)
10. Second user calls withdraw for 1e15 asdUSDC, amountToWithdraw = 1000
12. safeTransfer tries to send 1000 but contract only has 980 → REVERTS (line 61)
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The core vulnerability is at asdUSDC.sol:40 where usdcBalances[_usdcVersion] += _amount uses the requested transfer amount rather than the actual received amount. This creates a growing discrepancy between recorded and actual balances. The withdraw path at line 61 calls safeTransfer which will revert when actual balance < requested withdrawal. The recover function at line 73 also fails because the subtraction ERC20.balanceOf(this) - usdcBalances[version] underflows when usdcBalances > actual balance.

deployment_assumptions_checked: A fee-on-transfer USDC version is whitelisted by the owner
config_validation.deployment_evidence: deployASDRouter.js whitelists specific addresses (0x59a49a3646eC96339525B89bEB35c1c095cc1992 on canto, 0xa5bFA7E06eBc71Ca3cc58f5AC0954ebf6E9890E5 on canto-testnet). The scripts do not reveal whether these specific tokens have fee-on-transfer behavior. The owner can whitelist additional tokens at any time via updateWhitelist(). The Phase A analysis token semantics list fee_on_transfer as a possible behavior for USDC_variants.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack scenario is valid: (1) Owner whitelists a fee-on-transfer USDC variant. (2) Attacker deposits 1000 tokens, contract receives 990, records 1000. (3) Legitimate user deposits 1000 tokens, contract receives 990, records 2000 total. Actual balance: 1980. (4) Attacker withdraws 1000 asdUSDC worth = 1000 USDC. Actual balance drops to 980, recorded drops to 1000. (5) Legitimate user tries to withdraw 1000 USDC but contract only holds 980 → safeTransfer reverts. The attacker effectively extracted 10 tokens that belonged to the legitimate user. The attack profit scales linearly with the fee rate and deposit size.
validation_result.impact_assessment: If a fee-on-transfer USDC variant is whitelisted: (1) usdcBalances becomes permanently inflated above actual token holdings, (2) early withdrawers can withdraw their full amount at the expense of later withdrawers, (3) the last withdrawer(s) cannot withdraw due to insufficient actual balance, (4) the owner's recover() function cannot fix the discrepancy because it will underflow and revert, (5) the insolvency compounds with each deposit. This directly violates INV-ECON-003: ERC20(v).balanceOf(address(this)) >= usdcBalances[v].
recommended_mitigations: Use the balance-before/after pattern in deposit(): uint256 balBefore = ERC20(_usdcVersion).balanceOf(address(this)); safeTransferFrom(...); uint256 received = ERC20(_usdcVersion).balanceOf(address(this)) - balBefore; usdcBalances[_usdcVersion] += received;
Use the actual received amount for minting: amountToMint = received * (10 ** (this.decimals() - ERC20(_usdcVersion).decimals()));
Alternatively, explicitly reject fee-on-transfer tokens by requiring that the balance change equals _amount after transfer
Add a whitelist check to recover() to prevent recovery of non-whitelisted tokens
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy a mock ERC20 with 1% fee-on-transfer and 6 decimals. 2. Deploy ASDUSDC, whitelist the mock token. 3. Have Alice deposit 1000 tokens (contract receives 990, records 1000). 4. Have Bob deposit 1000 tokens (contract receives 990, records 2000; actual balance 1980). 5. Alice withdraws 1000 USDC worth of asdUSDC → succeeds (balance now 980, recorded 1000). 6. Bob withdraws 1000 USDC worth of asdUSDC → reverts (only 980 available). 7. Owner calls recover() → reverts due to underflow (usdcBalances 1000 > actual 980).
uncertainties: Whether any currently whitelisted USDC variant on Canto actually implements fee-on-transfer is unknown from the provided scripts
Future whitelist additions could introduce fee-on-transfer tokens even if current ones are standard
The exact fee rate of potential fee-on-transfer tokens affects the magnitude of the insolvency
additional_testing_needed: Verify the fee-on-transfer behavior of the specific USDC addresses whitelisted in deployment (0x59a49a3646eC96339525B89bEB35c1c095cc1992 on canto)
Test with varying fee rates to quantify compounding insolvency over multiple deposits
Confirm recover() underflow behavior with a Foundry test
